\subsection{Real-data headline experiments}

\input{tables/exp_021_holdout}

PennyLane \texttt{default.qubit} and \texttt{lightning.qubit} backends are
\textbf{parity-accepted} on breast cancer QNN: mean difference $-0.4$~pp,
Wilcoxon $p{=}0.844$, Cohen's~$d{=}{-}0.07$ (negligible).

\input{tables/exp_022_holdout}

Parameter-matched nano parity is \textbf{inconclusive}: positive mean gaps
($+0.5$~pp breast cancer, $+1.3$~pp wine) but Wilcoxon not Holm-significant
at $\alpha{=}0.05$ ($p{\geq}0.125$).

\input{tables/exp_024_holdout}

Flagship QuantumNano-BC (exp\_024) on full Wisconsin Breast Cancer is
\textbf{parity-accepted}: hybrid sandwich 97.4\% vs logistic regression 97.9\%
mean holdout ($\Delta{=}{-}0.5$~pp, within the 2~pp threshold). The advantage
claim ($\geq$3~pp) is rejected; logistic regression remains the recommended
shippable baseline.

\subsection{Conventional tabular baselines (HIGGS, exp\_058)}
\label{sec:exp058}

We compare the shipped \textbf{LargeNanoMLP} against four conventional classifiers on
open HIGGS tabular data (805K train / 172.5K val, 28 physics features).
Table~\ref{tab:exp_058} reports validation ROC-AUC; methodology and parameter parity
are in Table~\ref{tab:exp_058_methodology}; pairwise gains in
Tables~\ref{tab:exp_058_gains}--\ref{tab:exp_058_ci}.

\input{tables/exp_058_holdout}

\input{tables/exp_058_methodology}

\input{tables/exp_058_gains}

\paragraph{Headline comparison (our model vs best conventional deep net).}
Both LargeNanoMLP and sklearn \texttt{MLPClassifier} use \textbf{1{,}141{,}377 trainable
parameters} in identical layer widths ($2048{\rightarrow}512{\rightarrow}64$).
The architectural difference is regularization: our PyTorch stack applies \textbf{dropout
0.3} after every hidden ReLU block; sklearn's matched MLP uses \textbf{no dropout}.
Training also differs: LargeNanoMLP was trained in \texttt{exp\_032} with CUDA mini-batches
($\sim$108\,s for 12 epochs); the sklearn baseline retrains on CPU in 679\,s for the
same epoch budget (\texttt{max\_iter=12}, convergence warning emitted).

On full validation data the sklearn MLP reaches ROC-AUC \textbf{0.8429} vs
\textbf{0.8358} for our frozen checkpoint ($\Delta{=}{-}0.71$~pp favouring sklearn).
The pre-registered gate (nano $\geq$ best conventional + 0.5~pp) is therefore
\textbf{rejected} --- an honest negative result documented in \texttt{negative\_results.md}.

\paragraph{Gains vs weaker conventional baselines.}
LargeNanoMLP retains large margins over simpler stacks on the same val split:
\textbf{+15.09~pp} vs logistic regression (0.6849),
\textbf{+2.61~pp} vs HistGradientBoosting (0.8097), and
\textbf{+5.85~pp} vs shallow XGBoost (0.7773).
These gains confirm that deep nonlinear capacity matters on HIGGS; the open question
is whether the \emph{PyTorch} training recipe beats a \emph{parameter-matched sklearn MLP}
when both see all 805K rows --- on this run, sklearn wins narrowly.

\paragraph{CI slice (50K train).}
On a 50K-row smoke slice (Table~\ref{tab:exp_058_ci}), LargeNanoMLP leads by
\textbf{+4.14~pp} over the best conventional model (HistGradientBoosting, 0.7915).
Slice size therefore affects the headline: our checkpoint generalises well on subsampled
data but is slightly below a freshly trained full-data sklearn MLP.

\paragraph{Practical takeaway.}
LargeNanoMLP is the recommended serve model when inference latency matters (0.2\,s val
eval vs 679\,s sklearn retrain) and when logistic/GBDT baselines suffice.
For maximum val AUC on full HIGGS, a matched sklearn MLP currently edges our shipped
weights by 0.71~pp; retraining the PyTorch nano or ensembling remains future work.
This experiment does \textbf{not} involve quantum circuits --- it validates the
classical nano backbone against industry-standard tabular tooling.

\subsection{Four-anchor grand comparison (exp\_068)}
\label{sec:grand_comparison}

We synthesize publication $\Delta$~pp metrics across four classical nano anchors
(C1 HIGGS, C2 NIHR, C3 GoBug, C4 ACYD soybean) and quantum replication recipes
frozen on each anchor (Table~\ref{tab:grand_comparison}).
No quantum recipe simultaneously wins on $\geq 3/4$ domains at the pre-registered
+0.5~pp gate --- confirming the exp\_068 hypothesis.
LargeNanoMLP beats logistic on HIGGS (+14.09~pp) and ACYD (+3.86~pp) but not on
NIHR/GoBug PR-AUC gates; sklearn HistGradientBoosting edges the nano on ACYD val
($-$1.64~pp, exp\_061).
The QNN 4-qubit head remains within 1~pp of the frozen classical head on all four
domains (best: +0.04~pp HIGGS).

\input{tables/grand_comparison}

\subsection{Research Cycle v2 (agro frontier, quantum 2.0, multi-crop)}
\label{sec:cycle_v2}

Cycle~v2 publication runs on ACYD Brazil maize (RTX~4060) stress-tested nano
architectures against HistGradientBoosting, soft-label distillation, residual QNN
heads, Fourier re-upload encodings, Pauli/shadow feature maps, soy{+}maize joint
training, and hard temporal drift.
Tables~\ref{tab:boosting_frontier}--\ref{tab:hard_drift} summarize the registered
gates: distillation (exp\_092, $-0.48$~pp vs HistGB) and hard-drift ResidualNano
(exp\_094, $-0.61$~pp vs HistGB) are Cycle~v2 \textbf{accepts}; architecture search,
sample-efficiency, residual QNN, Fourier re-upload, shadow features, and multi-crop
joint training are honest negatives.

\input{tables/boosting_frontier}

\input{tables/sample_efficiency}

\input{tables/quantum_v2}

\input{tables/multicrop}

\input{tables/hard_drift}

\paragraph{Practical takeaway.}
The recommended maize serve model remains HistGB-distilled ResidualNano
(\texttt{residual\_nano\_distill\_acyd\_maize}). Shared multi-crop trunks,
Fourier climate re-upload heads, and Pauli/shadow feature banks do not improve
maize ranking on the temporal val split.

\subsection{Key findings}

On synthetic 2D tasks, parameter-matched classical MLPs remain competitive with
variational quantum circuits (exp\_001, exp\_008).
Entanglement topology shows no Holm-significant benefit on circles (exp\_003, exp\_009).
Curriculum staging and self-play provide no significant holdout gain (exp\_005, exp\_007).
